title: Dancin Homer

jokes.text: The only thing worse than being a loser... is being a guy telling the story of how he became a loser.
jokes.setup: Homer is reluctant to tell his Capital City story
jokes.punchline: Self-aware observation about the meta-embarrassment of recounting failure
jokes.explanation: Sharp meta-commentary with perfect Homer self-awareness. Economical and quotable opening that sets up the entire episode's framework.

jokes.text: Two birds with one stone! Okay, everybody out!
jokes.setup: Homer thinks he's lost his family while driving
jokes.punchline: Immediately realizes they're at their destination and acts like it was intentional
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness followed by fake confidence. Good character beat but fairly predictable.

jokes.text: Lisa, you're forgetting the beer. It comes in 72-ounce tubs.
jokes.setup: Lisa poetically describes the ballpark atmosphere
jokes.punchline: Homer reduces it all to beer served in comically large containers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - ignoring poetry for practical beer information. The specific '72-ounce tubs' detail makes it memorable.

jokes.text: Marge, this ticket doesn't just give me a seat. It also gives me the right, no, the duty to make a complete ass of myself.
jokes.setup: Marge warns Homer to space out his beer consumption after last year's incident
jokes.punchline: Homer frames obnoxious behavior as a civic responsibility
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from 'right' to 'duty' - Homer's twisted logic at its finest. The formal language makes the absurdity funnier.

jokes.text: Well, if it isn't the Simps.
jokes.setup: Burns greets the Simpson family
jokes.punchline: Shortens 'Simpsons' to 'Simps' - unintentionally insulting
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective Burns moment - his oblivious condescension. Economy saves it from being too obvious.

jokes.text: Oh, and these must be Bart, Lisa and Expecting.
jokes.setup: Burns reading from an outdated card about the Simpson family
jokes.punchline: Refers to baby Maggie as 'Expecting' since the card is from when Marge was pregnant
jokes.explanation: Clever bureaucratic absurdity. Burns' detachment from employees made funnier by the outdated information.

jokes.text: The baby's name isn't important. Let's go.
jokes.setup: Smithers suggests updating the card with the baby's real name
jokes.punchline: Burns dismisses the baby's identity entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfectly efficient Burns callousness. The casual dismissal of a baby's importance is peak Burns character work.

jokes.text: Lousy, washed-up, broken-down tub of guts! Who does he think he is?
jokes.setup: Former baseball star Flash Bailor refuses to sign Homer's ball
jokes.punchline: Homer's over-the-top angry reaction to a simple autograph refusal
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer tantrum. The 'tub of guts' insult is colorful but the beat is predictable.

jokes.text: Springfield Kozy Kort Motel, Room 26. How about it? Flash.
jokes.setup: Marge asks Flash to sign the ball for her son
jokes.punchline: Flash writes a sleazy proposition instead of an autograph
jokes.explanation: Dark turn that subverts the innocent autograph request. The specific motel details make it more uncomfortable and funny.

jokes.text: Wow, Flash Bailor came on to my wife! You've still got the magic, Marge.
jokes.setup: Most husbands would be angry about a man propositioning their wife
jokes.punchline: Homer is actually impressed and compliments Marge
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - completely missing the point and being proud instead of jealous. Character-driven absurdity at its best.

jokes.text: Examine my zipper? Why?
jokes.setup: Marge says 'X-Y-Z' to indicate Homer's zipper is down
jokes.punchline: Homer doesn't understand the code and asks for clarification
jokes.explanation: Standard 'character doesn't get obvious reference' joke. Homer's obliviousness is expected here.

jokes.text: Spit on this for me, Smithers. One hawker coming up, sir.
jokes.setup: Burns is about to throw out the first pitch
jokes.punchline: Burns has Smithers spit on the baseball like an old-timey pitcher
jokes.explanation: Good Burns/Smithers dynamic - Burns' old-fashioned baseball knowledge combined with making Smithers do gross tasks.

jokes.text: What a lame-o! I could hear the air being torn, sir. Oh, shut up!
jokes.setup: Burns throws a terrible first pitch
jokes.punchline: Homer mocks Burns while Smithers tries to flatter him, Burns tells Smithers to shut up
jokes.explanation: Three-way character dynamic works well. Burns' embarrassment overrides his usual acceptance of Smithers' flattery.

jokes.text: Hey, Burns! Hey, rag arm! You throw like my sister! Yeah, you throw like me!
jokes.setup: Homer continues to heckle Burns' pitching
jokes.punchline: Lisa joins in but accidentally insults herself
jokes.explanation: Lisa's innocent participation backfires perfectly. Good callback structure with her not realizing she's the sister being referenced.

jokes.text: How about that? We're in the record book!
jokes.setup: Announcer mentions the Isotopes have the longest losing streak in pro baseball
jokes.punchline: Homer is genuinely excited about this dubious distinction
jokes.explanation: Homer's ability to find the positive in complete failure. Shows his optimistic obliviousness perfectly.

jokes.text: It just means you can't wave your fanny in public. Rub it in.
jokes.setup: Homer is upset about sitting next to Burns
jokes.punchline: Marge's practical explanation makes Homer feel worse, and he sees it as her gloating
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Homer's self-pity and Marge's practical nature. The 'rub it in' reaction is believably petulant.

jokes.text: Not a chance. Only idiots drink beer.
jokes.setup: Homer asks if Burns wants beer
jokes.punchline: Burns insults beer drinkers right in front of Homer, a known beer lover
jokes.explanation: Burns' oblivious insult to Homer is perfectly in character. The economy and timing work well for the awkwardness.

jokes.text: If someone of your stature can enjoy a beer, maybe I'm wrong on the subject.
jokes.setup: Burns just called beer drinkers idiots
jokes.punchline: Burns immediately reverses his position when he decides to drink with Homer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns hypocrisy - his principles are instantly flexible. The formal language makes his flip-flopping funnier.

jokes.text: The hitter's off his rocker, kissing Betty Crocker!
jokes.setup: Burns tries to participate in crowd heckling
jokes.punchline: Uses outdated, nonsensical rhyming insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns antiquated humor - the dated reference and forced rhyme scheme show his disconnect from modern times.

jokes.text: Little baby batter, can't control his bladder!
jokes.setup: Homer is getting drunk and heckling
jokes.punchline: Crude rhyming insult about the batter
jokes.explanation: Crude but shows Homer getting more obnoxious as he drinks. The rhyme structure works but it's pretty lowbrow.

jokes.text: What do you say we freshen up our little drinkie-poos?
jokes.setup: Burns is getting drunk with Homer
jokes.punchline: Burns uses cutesy language for getting more beer
jokes.explanation: Burns' posh, infantilizing language for beer is perfectly in character. The contrast between his usual formality and 'drinkie-poos' works well.

jokes.text: Well, Simpsie, up for another wave? All right, Burnsie.
jokes.setup: Burns and Homer are now drunk and bonding
jokes.punchline: They're using cutesy nicknames for each other
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of their unlikely friendship. The diminutive nicknames show how alcohol has broken down social barriers.

jokes.text: These banjos couldn't carry Pie Traynor's glove.
jokes.setup: The team is playing terribly
jokes.punchline: Announcer uses obscure old-timey baseball reference to insult current players
jokes.explanation: Good baseball-specific humor. The obscure reference and 'banjos' slang shows writing depth, though may go over some heads.

jokes.text: As I got up, I felt an intoxication that had nothing to do with alcohol. It was the intoxication of being a public spectacle.
jokes.setup: Homer is about to start dancing
jokes.punchline: Philosophical observation about his exhibitionist nature
jokes.explanation: Surprisingly eloquent and self-aware Homer moment. The contrast between the poetic language and his basic attention-seeking is excellent.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! The Isotopes win a game! The Isotopes win a game!
jokes.setup: The terrible team finally scores
jokes.punchline: Announcer is shocked that they won even one game
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the losing streak setup. The announcer's disbelief sells how rare this is for the team.

jokes.text: But Simpson's shameless display of exhibitionism tainted the evening. He's banned for life from all company outings.
jokes.setup: Burns just spent the evening getting drunk and bonding with Homer
jokes.punchline: Burns immediately turns on Homer and bans him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns hypocrisy - he was part of the 'shameless display' but immediately distances himself. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: Me? A mascot for a bush-league team?
jokes.setup: Team owner offers Homer a mascot job
jokes.punchline: Homer acts like it's beneath him despite being unemployed and desperate for attention
jokes.explanation: Good Homer false pride moment. His standards are hilariously inappropriate given his circumstances.

jokes.text: If I hadn't thought of a nickname, all our lives might've been spared.
jokes.setup: Homer reflects on the pivotal moment
jokes.punchline: He recognizes that creating 'Dancing Homer' led to their downfall
jokes.explanation: Great dramatic irony and self-awareness. Homer understanding the exact moment his hubris doomed the family is surprisingly poignant.

jokes.text: For the first time, people didn't laugh at me. They laughed towards me.
jokes.setup: Homer reflecting on his mascot success
jokes.punchline: His poetic but slightly wrong description of positive attention
jokes.explanation: Brilliant, quotable line that perfectly captures Homer's simple wisdom and need for validation. The slight grammatical awkwardness makes it more endearing.

jokes.text: I'll stick this where the sun don't shine. Oh yeah? And where might that be?
jokes.setup: Bart threatens to put the T-shirt somewhere inappropriate
jokes.punchline: Homer doesn't understand the euphemism and asks for clarification
jokes.explanation: Standard 'Homer doesn't get innuendo' joke. Well-executed but predictable setup/punchline structure.

jokes.text: I'm in a Caribbean mood tonight. Give me 'Baby Elephant Walk' with a little reggae beat.
jokes.setup: Homer is DJing his own entrance music
jokes.punchline: He requests a weirdly specific musical mashup
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist Homer moment. The specific song choice and reggae combination is randomly specific enough to be funny.

jokes.text: You can't fire the players, so you fire the mascot! You make me sick.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks he's being fired
jokes.punchline: His indignant but actually reasonable analysis of scapegoating
jokes.explanation: Homer makes a surprisingly astute point about sports management. The righteous indignation works because he's actually correct.

jokes.text: Because they might say no.
jokes.setup: Owner suggests Homer talk to his family about moving to Capital City
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest admission that he won't consult them
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic and selfishness. The economy and honesty make it funnier than a more elaborate explanation would be.

jokes.text: This was a big decision. I should've listened to my kids, not my dumb wife. I shouldn't call her that. Bite my tongue.
jokes.setup: Homer reflecting on his decision-making
jokes.punchline: He insults Marge, immediately regrets it, but not enough to actually change
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's stream of consciousness and superficial guilt. The self-correction is hollow but believable.

jokes.text: It's not so bad. You'll die someplace else. What do I do about friends? You'll make new, better friends.
jokes.setup: Homer expresses fear about leaving Springfield
jokes.punchline: Barney's casual dismissal of Homer's death concerns and insulting of current friends
jokes.explanation: Great dark humor with Barney's matter-of-fact approach to mortality and casual insult to their friendship. Very character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Yours is dancing on dugouts.
jokes.setup: Homer talks about everyone having a calling from God
jokes.punchline: He identifies his divine purpose as dancing on dugouts
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer grandiosity - treating his silly dancing as a divine calling. The specificity of 'dugouts' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Sure. What would you like? Four, five years?
jokes.setup: Homer asks for time off to pursue his mascot career
jokes.punchline: Burns sarcastically offers him an absurdly long vacation
jokes.explanation: Good Burns sarcasm showing his disdain for Homer. The specific time offers make it more cutting than generic dismissal.

jokes.text: I'm gonna miss you too. Not.
jokes.setup: Emotional goodbye between Homer and Barney
jokes.punchline: Barney uses the 'Not!' joke format to undercut the sentiment
jokes.explanation: Period-appropriate 'Not!' joke that fits Barney's character but relies on a tired '90s trope.

jokes.text: Milhouse, this way, we'll be friends forever.
jokes.setup: Bart is saying goodbye to Milhouse
jokes.punchline: Bart frames leaving as a way to preserve their friendship
jokes.explanation: Sweet Bart logic - avoiding relationship decay through separation. Shows his emotional intelligence hiding behind cynicism.

jokes.text: If we had known each other better, my leaving would actually mean something.
jokes.setup: Homer is saying goodbye to a neighbor
jokes.punchline: He admits their relationship is too shallow for meaningful goodbye
jokes.explanation: Brutally honest and surprisingly insightful. Homer's casual acknowledgment of superficial relationships is both funny and a bit sad.

jokes.text: Some may say that I have been given a bad break in life. Little education, bald as a cue ball, 10 years on the same job for the same salary. But today, as I leave for Capital City, I consider myself the luckiest mascot on the face of the earth.
jokes.setup: Homer delivers what sounds like a serious speech
jokes.punchline: Parodies Lou Gehrig's famous farewell speech but for becoming a mascot
jokes.explanation: Masterful parody of Lou Gehrig's speech, perfectly adapted to Homer's circumstances. The contrast between the noble original and mascot context is brilliant.

jokes.text: Kids, look! Street crime.
jokes.setup: Family is touring Capital City and seeing the sights
jokes.punchline: Homer treats witnessing a crime like a tourist attraction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer naivety - treating urban decay as entertainment. The economy and deadpan delivery make it particularly effective.

jokes.text: For the first time ever...Marge fell asleep before I did.
jokes.setup: Homer is nervous about his big performance
jokes.punchline: Homer notices role reversal in their marriage dynamic
jokes.explanation: Subtle character observation about relationship dynamics. Shows Homer's awareness of change and his nervousness about the performance.

jokes.text: Bart was strangely quiet. Later, he said he was confused by his respect for me. It wouldn't last.
jokes.setup: Homer narrates Bart's unusual behavior
jokes.punchline: Homer acknowledges that Bart's respect is temporary
jokes.explanation: Great character insight showing both Homer's self-awareness and the unusual father-son dynamic. The matter-of-fact delivery of 'It wouldn't last' is perfect.

jokes.text: It's also the inning I wish I had a zipper in the front.
jokes.setup: Goofball explains the importance of the 5th inning
jokes.punchline: Reveals practical costume problem with inappropriate directness
jokes.explanation: Mildly crude humor about mascot costume practicalities. Gets a reaction but isn't particularly sophisticated.

jokes.text: It's also the inning I wish I had a zipper in the front.
jokes.setup: Goofball explains the importance of the 5th inning
jokes.punchline: He mentions a very practical bathroom-related costume problem
jokes.explanation: Practical bathroom humor that shows the unglamorous reality behind mascot life. Fits the character but isn't especially clever.

jokes.text: This section is for the ex-wives.
jokes.setup: Marge is confused about their distant seats
jokes.punchline: The bad seats are specifically designated for ex-wives
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about baseball player relationships. The casual institutional sexism is efficiently revealed and funny.

jokes.text: I was graceful. I was witty. Brother, I was something! But they didn't care.
jokes.setup: Homer describing his performance with confidence
jokes.punchline: The brutal reality that his best effort meant nothing
jokes.explanation: Captures the crushing disappointment of performing your best for an indifferent audience

jokes.text: He doesn't make me want to cheer. I pity him, making a fool of himself. These cornball antics don't play in Capital City!
jokes.setup: Homer performs his routine for the big city crowd
jokes.punchline: The sophisticated audience is not impressed and finds him embarrassing
jokes.explanation: Painful but necessary character moment showing the difference between small-town and big-city audiences. The cringe is well-earned.

jokes.text: I can't! Not after I've seen Capital City! I'll wither and die like a flower.
jokes.setup: Homer is told to go back to Springfield after failing
jokes.punchline: Homer's melodramatic refusal using flowery metaphors
jokes.explanation: Homer's dramatic side comes out in defeat. The flower metaphor is overwrought in a way that fits his character.

jokes.text: As my son would say, I'm one sad, ape-like dude.
jokes.setup: Homer concludes his story
jokes.punchline: He uses Bart's vocabulary to describe himself, including the ape comparison
jokes.explanation: Good character moment where Homer adopts Bart's slang, including the unflattering description. Shows their relationship dynamic.

jokes.text: I wonder why stories of degradation and humiliation make you more popular.
jokes.setup: Everyone is hanging on Homer's every word during his failure story
jokes.punchline: Homer makes a surprisingly astute observation about human nature and storytelling
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on both the episode's structure and human psychology. Homer's accidental wisdom about failure being more compelling than success is profound.

tmdb_overview: Homer fires up the crowd at a Springfield Isotopes baseball game and is chosen to be the team's new mascot. He immediately becomes a popular attraction and the Isotopes start a winning streak. As a result, Homer is promoted to a team in Capital City, the Capital City Capitals.

guest_stars.name: Pamela Hayden
guest_stars.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Janey Powell / Ex-Wife (voice)

insight: Homer's dancing obsession carries 52 jokes at moderate density, balancing character comedy with sparse standouts.
